script:
export const meta = {
  name: 'compose-throughput-forensics',
  description: 'Line-by-line Opus investigation of what ACTUALLY caps compose throughput. 8 hypothesis lenses, adversarial verification, Fable synthesis. Find every serialization bug, not just the one we already know.',
  phases: [
    { title: 'Read', detail: '8 Opus investigators, one per failure hypothesis, line-by-line' },
    { title: 'Refute', detail: 'adversarially attack each finding — default to REFUTED' },
    { title: 'Synthesize', detail: 'Fable: ranked list of what really caps throughput', model: 'fable' },
  ],
}

const CONTEXT = `
POLARIS COMPOSE — THE INVESTIGATION.

WHAT COMPOSE DOES: renders 346 baskets into a deep research report. Compose model = z-ai/glm-5.2 via
OpenRouter. Each rendered sentence is verified by an NLI entailment judge (also via OpenRouter, SAME
account). Goal: render ALL 346 baskets fast. Today it is far too slow.

WORKTREE (read here): /home/polaris/wt/compose   — also /workspace/POLARIS for reference
KEY FILES: src/polaris_graph/generator/abstractive_writer.py, generator/quantified_analysis.py,
  the NLI/entailment judge + provenance_generator, scripts/run_s5_i3.py, launch_compose_gear_*.sh

WHAT IS ALREADY KNOWN — DO NOT RE-FIND THIS, GO PAST IT:
Commit 0615bc5 fixed ONE serialization bug: _draft_passes_wrapper (abstractive_writer.py:661) was a
sync \`def\` called with NO \`await\` from the async coroutine _pre_pass_one_basket (:749), at two call
sites. It ran writer_verify_fn -> verify_sentence_provenance -> entailment_judge.judge() ->
_post_with_total_deadline -> fut.result(timeout=...) which BLOCKS THE CALLING THREAD — and the calling
thread was the asyncio event loop. So every NLI judge POST froze the whole compose loop and achieved
concurrency was 1, regardless of PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 / PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16.
Both call sites now \`await asyncio.to_thread(...)\`. Measured A/B: 2.06s -> 0.60s on 4 baskets.

THE OPERATOR'S QUESTION, AND THE POINT OF THIS INVESTIGATION:
"The 48-way parallelism was never real. One missing await is a suspiciously tidy explanation. What
ELSE is capping compose? Is the context too large? Not enough token budget? Crashes? Something else?"
Your job is to find the REMAINING throughput killers. Assume there are more. There usually are.

RULES OF EVIDENCE (this is the whole game):
- A finding is a MECHANISM at file:line, not a vibe. "Context might be big" is worthless.
  "prompt is built at :412 by concatenating all N basket digests with no cap, so a 346-basket run
  sends ~X tokens per call, and TTFT scales linearly with it" is a finding.
- READ THE ACTUAL CODE. Do not reason from the summary above. Open the files.
- Where you can, PROVE it — a tiny probe script in /tmp, a python -c, a log grep with counts.
  A measured number beats a code-reading every time.
- Report REAL output. If you find nothing in your lane, say "nothing found" — a clean lane is a
  useful result. Do NOT invent a finding to look productive.
- NEVER print the OpenRouter API key.
`

const FINDING_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  required: ['lane', 'findings'],
  properties: {
    lane: { type: 'string' },
    findings: {
      type: 'array',
      items: {
        type: 'object',
        required: ['title', 'file_line', 'mechanism', 'throughput_impact', 'evidence'],
        properties: {
          title: { type: 'string' },
          file_line: { type: 'string', description: 'exact file:line' },
          mechanism: { type: 'string', description: 'HOW it costs throughput, concretely' },
          throughput_impact: { enum: ['CRITICAL_serializes', 'HIGH', 'MEDIUM', 'LOW'] },
          evidence: { type: 'string', description: 'the code you read / the number you measured / the log lines' },
          measured: { type: 'boolean', description: 'true only if you PROVED it by running something' },
          fix_sketch: { type: 'string' },
        },
      },
    },
    nothing_found: { type: 'boolean' },
    notes: { type: 'string' },
  },
}

const REFUTE_SCHEMA = {
  type: 'object',
  required: ['refuted', 'reason'],
  properties: {
    refuted: { type: 'boolean', description: 'TRUE if the finding does not hold up. Default to TRUE when uncertain.' },
    reason: { type: 'string' },
    real_impact: { type: 'string', description: 'if it survives: what does it ACTUALLY cost, quantified' },
  },
}

// Eight lenses. Each is blind to the others — that is the point.
const LANES = [
  {
    key: 'more-event-loop-blocks',
    prompt: `LANE: OTHER EVENT-LOOP BLOCKS. The 0615bc5 bug was a sync call blocking the event loop.
FIND EVERY OTHER INSTANCE OF THAT BUG CLASS. Systematically hunt, across the whole compose path, for:
  - sync def called without await from inside an async def
  - .result() / .wait() / fut.result(timeout=) on a future, called on the event loop thread
  - time.sleep() (not asyncio.sleep) anywhere in an async path
  - requests/httpx SYNC client calls inside async code
  - blocking file I/O or sqlite calls on the loop (checkpoint writes! ev_store reads!)
  - threading.Lock / RLock acquired on the loop thread and held across an I/O call
Grep hard: 'def _', 'async def', '.result(', 'time.sleep', 'requests.', 'httpx.Client'.
Cross-reference every sync function called from every async one. This is the highest-yield lane —
the bug class already proved itself once. PROVE any hit: show the async caller AND the sync callee.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'context-size',
    prompt: `LANE: CONTEXT / PROMPT SIZE (the operator explicitly asked about this).
Trace EXACTLY what goes into each compose LLM call. Is the prompt built by concatenating all basket
digests / all evidence / the whole outline into every call? Is there a cap? MEASURE the real prompt
token count for a realistic 346-basket run — actually build one and count (tiktoken or len//4).
Questions: does prompt size scale with corpus size (O(N) per call = O(N^2) total)? Is the same huge
context re-sent on every one of hundreds of calls with no caching? Does GLM-5.2 prompt-cache apply
and are we structuring the prompt to hit it (stable prefix first)? Big context = slow TTFT + high
cost + more 429 weight (rate limits are often TOKEN-based, not request-based — check that).`,
  },
  {
    key: 'token-budget-truncation',
    prompt: `LANE: TOKEN BUDGET / TRUNCATION / RETRY-ON-TRUNCATION (operator asked about this too).
Find max_tokens / max_output / context-cap settings on every compose + judge call. Are we starving
the model (too-low max_tokens -> truncated output -> the code retries or discards -> wasted calls)?
Known related bug in this repo: "un-starve to GLM-5.2 real 131072 cap" (commit ecda022) — so
starvation HAS happened here before. Check: is there a truncation-detect -> retry loop? How often
does it fire in the REAL logs? Every retry is a doubled call. Also: reasoning-token truncation on
GLM-5.2 (the outline wheel has a documented 'GLM-5.2 reasoning truncation' failure). Count real
occurrences in logs, do not speculate.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'crash-retry-resume',
    prompt: `LANE: CRASHES, RETRIES, RESUME THRASH (operator asked: "crash?").
Read the REAL run logs for the recent s5/compose runs (outputs/s5_*, logs/, ckpt dirs). Count:
crashes, restarts, exceptions, retry storms, and whether the SELF-RELAUNCH launcher
(launch_compose_gear_*.sh, 'self-relaunch launcher' per commit 4e0b1f9) is looping — re-doing work
it already did. Check the SHA-gated section-level RESUME (commit 4e0b1f9): does it actually skip
completed sections, or does it silently redo them? A resume that re-renders finished work is a 2x+
throughput loss and looks like 'slow' rather than 'broken'. Also check for the two root-owned runs
self-contending. QUANTIFY from real logs: how many baskets got rendered more than once?`,
  },
  {
    key: 'concurrency-knobs-inert',
    prompt: `LANE: DO THE CONCURRENCY KNOBS ACTUALLY BIND?
PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY, PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM, PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY (default 4).
For EACH: trace from the env read to the actual semaphore/gather that uses it. Prove whether it is
LIVE on the compose path or INERT. (Precedent: the side-judge knob was documented as non-binding on
the compose path but binding elsewhere — knobs in this repo lie.) Look for: a semaphore created but
never awaited; a gather() over a list of size 1; a nested semaphore where the inner one is tighter
and silently dominates; a global cap that undercuts the per-stage cap. Find the SMALLEST effective
cap in the chain — that is the real concurrency, and it is probably much lower than anyone thinks.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'sequential-seams',
    prompt: `LANE: SEQUENTIAL SEAMS / HIDDEN BARRIERS.
Compose renders 346 baskets across sections. Map the ACTUAL control flow: what is parallel and what
is a barrier? Look for: a per-section await that waits for ALL baskets before starting the next
section (so wall-clock = sum of slowest-per-section, not max); a for-loop with await inside (classic
accidental serialization — should be asyncio.gather); passes that run strictly in sequence
(draft -> verify -> dedup -> coherence) when they could pipeline; a single-threaded post-processing
pass over all output. Draw the real execution graph and identify where the parallelism actually
collapses to 1. Cite line numbers for every await-in-a-loop you find.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'judge-verifier-path',
    prompt: `LANE: THE NLI JUDGE / VERIFIER — the high-call-volume half.
Every rendered sentence gets NLI-verified, so the judge is called FAR more than the writer. Read the
judge path end to end: entailment_judge.judge(), _post_with_total_deadline, provenance_generator,
judge_verdict_cache. Questions: is the judge called per SENTENCE (N calls) when it could be batched
(1 call, N pairs)? Is judge_verdict_cache actually hit, or does a key mismatch make the hit rate ~0
(MEASURE the hit rate)? Is there a per-call deadline/timeout that is way too long (a 300s deadline on
a hung call holds a slot for 5 minutes)? Is the ThreadPool bounded far tighter than the writer?
Provider rotation / total-deadline retry: does a slow provider stall the pool? Batching the judge is
potentially a 10x+ win — check whether the API supports it and whether we use it.`,
  },
  {
    key: 'measure-it-live',
    prompt: `LANE: JUST MEASURE IT. Everyone else is reading code; you RUN it.
Do a REAL, INSTRUMENTED compose run on a small basket set in /home/polaris/wt/compose (foreground,
hard timeout, e.g. \`timeout 900 python ...\`; NEVER nohup+tail -f). Instrument to answer:
  - What is the ACHIEVED concurrency over time (how many LLM calls actually in flight)? Sample it.
  - Where does the wall-clock actually GO? Break it down: writer calls vs judge calls vs local CPU
    vs waiting on a lock vs retry/backoff sleep. A flame-ish breakdown beats any theory.
  - Is the event loop still ever starved post-fix? Run a heartbeat coroutine (sleep 0.01 in a loop)
    and log the max gap. Gap >> 0.01s = something is STILL blocking the loop.
  - Actual req/s hitting OpenRouter, and how many 429s come back.
CAVEAT: root-owned run_s5_i3.py (PID 966951) may be competing for the same OpenRouter account —
check \`ps -p 966951\` and if it is alive, SAY your numbers are contaminated. Report REAL numbers.`,
  },
]

phase('Read')
// pipeline: each lane's findings get refuted as soon as that lane returns — no barrier
const perLane = await pipeline(
  LANES,
  lane => agent(`${CONTEXT}\n\n=== YOUR LANE ===\n${lane.prompt}`, {
    label: `read:${lane.key}`,
    phase: 'Read',
    schema: FINDING_SCHEMA,
  }),
  (res, lane) => {
    if (!res || res.nothing_found || !(res.findings ?? []).length) return []
    return parallel(
      res.findings.map(f => () =>
        agent(
          `${CONTEXT}

YOU ARE A REFUTER. An investigator claims the following caps compose throughput. Your job is to KILL
it. Open the real code and try to prove it WRONG. Default to refuted=true if you are uncertain — a
plausible-but-false finding sends the whole team down a dead end, which is worse than missing one.

CLAIM: ${f.title}
AT: ${f.file_line}
MECHANISM: ${f.mechanism}
CLAIMED IMPACT: ${f.throughput_impact}
THEIR EVIDENCE: ${f.evidence}
THEY ${f.measured ? 'MEASURED it' : 'DID NOT measure it — only read code'}

Attack it: Is that code path even reached at runtime? Is it already guarded/fixed elsewhere? Is the
impact real at 346-basket scale or negligible? Is it dominated by a bigger cost? If it survives,
quantify what it ACTUALLY costs.`,
          { label: `refute:${lane.key}`, phase: 'Refute', schema: REFUTE_SCHEMA },
        ).then(v => ({ ...f, lane: lane.key, verdict: v })),
      ),
    )
  },
)

const survivors = perLane
  .flat()
  .filter(Boolean)
  .filter(f => f.verdict && !f.verdict.refuted)

log(`survivors after refutation: ${survivors.length}`)

phase('Synthesize')
const synthesis = await agent(
  `${CONTEXT}

YOU ARE FABLE. Eight independent Opus investigators read the compose path line-by-line. Every finding
was then attacked by an adversarial refuter. These are the ones that SURVIVED:

${JSON.stringify(survivors, null, 2)}

Answer the operator's actual question: WHY IS COMPOSE SLOW, really?

1. RANK the surviving causes by real throughput cost at 346-basket scale. Put a number on each where
   you can. Be explicit about which are MEASURED and which are only code-read — the operator has
   already been burned once by a confident unmeasured thesis (the "429 is the ceiling" claim in the
   master brief, which was wrong because concurrency was actually 1).
2. Answer the operator's three named hypotheses directly and by name: is it CONTEXT TOO LARGE? is it
   TOKEN BUDGET? is it CRASHES? Yes or no, with evidence, for each.
3. THE ONE THING: if we fix exactly one thing tomorrow, what is it, and what is the expected speedup?
4. What is the honest expected wall-clock to render all 346 baskets after the top fix — and what does
   that estimate rest on?
5. What did we NOT establish? Name the gaps. An honest "we still cannot explain X" is required if
   true — do not close the loop with a story that merely sounds complete.`,
  { label: 'fable:why-is-compose-slow', phase: 'Synthesize', model: 'fable', effort: 'high' },
)

return { survivors_count: survivors.length, survivors, synthesis }

survivors.title: SYNTH_PRIMARY re-draft blocks the event loop for up to 330 seconds per basket via fut.result()
survivors.mechanism: `_make_group_redraft_fn._redraft` is a SYNC closure threaded into `_compose_one_basket`'s bounded repair loop (`_synth_primary_repair_loop`, verified_compose.py:2136 `fresh = redraft_fn(...)`). It does `fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run()))` then `fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0)` — a blocking wait for a FULL GLM-5.2 writer call. Because `_compose_section_per_basket` itself runs on the event loop (finding 1), this `fut.result()` blocks the event loop for the entire writer call, bounded only at call_deadline_s+30 = 300+30 = 330 SECONDS. It fires up to PG_WRITER_REPAIR_MAX (default 2) times per basket, for every basket whose pre-pass draft failed verification. The function's own docstring states it 'runs synchronously inside the already-running section event loop'.
survivors.evidence:
multi_section_generator.py:4994-4996:
        fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run()))
        try:
            result = str(fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0) or "")
call_deadline_s = _env_float(PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CALL_DEADLINE_S, 120.0); launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh:36 sets it to 300. PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY=1 (iter5:22) and _vc_redraft_fn is non-None only when _synth_primary_active, so this path IS live. verified_compose.py:1739 _writer_repair_max default 2.
survivors.fix_sketch: Make the repair loop async, or (minimal) hoist the whole `_compose_section_per_basket` call off-loop per finding 1 — then _redraft's fut.result() blocks a worker thread instead of the loop. Do NOT leave a 330s .result() reachable from the loop thread.

verdict.reason:
The mechanism is real, reachable, and blocks the event loop — I could not refute it.

CONFIRMED by reading the actual code:
1. Call site runs ON the loop. `_compose_section_per_basket` is invoked as a plain synchronous call (no `await`, no `to_thread`) at multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514, inside `async def _run_section` (:5182). I verified there is no thread offload wrapping it. The pre-pass above it (:5448 `await abstractive_pre_pass`) is properly awaited, but the compose call is not.
2. The redraft path is LIVE under iter5. launch_compose_gear_iter5.sh sets PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY=1; `_vc_redraft_fn = _make_group_redraft_fn(...)` is non-None when `_synth_primary_active` (:5464); it is threaded into `_compose_section_per_basket(redraft_fn=...)` (:5475).
3. The blocking wait is real. `_synth_primary_repair_loop` (verified_compose.py:2136) calls `fresh = redraft_fn(...)` synchronously; `_redraft` (multi_section_generator.py:4969) does `fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run()))` then `fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0)` (:4994-4996). `fut.result()` blocks the CALLING thread, which is the event-loop thread. The function's own docstring (:4938-4940) explicitly states it "runs synchronously inside the already-running section event loop" and uses a ThreadPoolExecutor precisely BECAUSE `asyncio.run` cannot nest in a running loop — the design ACKNOWLEDGES it blocks the loop.
4. Blocking the loop actually costs concurrency. `_gather_sections_isolated` (verified_compose... msg:1003-1005) drives all sections via `asyncio.ensure_future` + `asyncio.gather`, so multiple `_run_section` coroutines share one loop. Any synchronous block in one section freezes every other section.

This is the SAME defect class as the already-fixed finding-1 (sync blocking network call executed on the event-loop thread), but on the redraft path, which the 0615bc5 fix did NOT touch.

Kill attempts that FAILED: (a) no `to_thread`/thread offload wraps the compose call; (b) the design deliberately runs it on the loop; (c) sections are genuinely concurrent so the block has a cost.

Honest caveats (which limit magnitude but do not refute the mechanism): redraft fires ONLY for baskets whose pre-pass draft had ≥1 sentence FAIL verification (not all 346), so frequency depends on the verify-failure rate, which I could not measure — the I-rdy-018 live logs showed 0 firings but predate/don't exercise this slate. Also it is one contributor inside an already-fully-synchronous `_compose_section_per_basket` (per-sentence verify also runs on the loop), so it is not the SOLE cap. But those bound the impact, they do not make the claim false.
verdict.real_impact: Each redraft is a LIVE GLM-5.2 group-writer call (PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS=131072, REASONING_MAX_TOKENS=65536, CALL_DEADLINE_S=300) run via fut.result() on the event-loop thread, firing up to PG_WRITER_REPAIR_MAX=2 times per basket whose pre-pass draft failed verification. While it blocks, the whole loop — every concurrently-gathered section — is frozen. Worst-case block per call = call_deadline_s+30 = 330s (only on a wedge); typical block = one full writer-call latency, realistically seconds to tens of seconds given the 65536-token reasoning budget (NOT the ~0.6s cached pre-pass latency, because redraft is a fresh network call, not a dict lookup). At 346-basket scale, if even ~15-20% of baskets fail verification (~50-70 baskets) × up to 2 redrafts × ~10-30s each = hundreds to >1000s of pure serialized event-loop blocking during which zero cross-section parallelism exists. The "330s per basket" figure is the true upper bound (the timeout wall), not the expected per-basket cost. Fix mirrors finding-1: wrap the redraft invocation in `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` (or make the repair loop / compose async), so the writer call runs off the loop.

survivors.title: Every NLI judge POST is gated by a process-global BoundedSemaphore(4) — acquired ON the event loop thread
survivors.mechanism: `_post_with_total_deadline` opens with `with _acquire_judge_slot():` BEFORE submitting the POST. `acquire_judge_slot` resolves PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY, which is set in NO launch script, so it falls to DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4, and does a blocking `sem.acquire()` on a process-global threading.BoundedSemaphore. Two consequences: (a) at most 4 entailment POSTs are in flight process-wide no matter that PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 and PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 — the fixed pre-pass's 12 verify threads immediately queue 8-deep behind a cap of 4, so the 0615bc5 speedup is itself capped; (b) on the compose leg (finding 1) the thread doing sem.acquire() IS the event loop, so slot-queueing time is loop-freeze time on top of POST time.
survivors.evidence: Measured under the iter5 env: `resolve_max_concurrency() == 4`, `DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY == 4`. `grep -c PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY launch_compose_gear_iter{1,3,4,5}.sh scripts/run_s5_i3.py` -> 0 in every file. judge_concurrency.py:178 `sem.acquire()` (no timeout on the binding callers).
survivors.fix_sketch: Set PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY explicitly in the launch script (it was sized at 4 for a 4-host mirror chain during a 429 storm; with the compose leg off-loop, raise it to match PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM and watch 429 rate). Independently: never call acquire_judge_slot from the loop thread.

verdict.reason:
The core mechanism is CONFIRMED, not refuted. I verified: (1) `resolve_max_concurrency()` returns 4 under the iter5 env (`DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4`, PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY unset) — measured directly. (2) The path is reached at runtime: default `_entailment_mode()` is 'enforce' (strict_verify.py:369, "I-bug-095: default mode is 'enforce'"), so every writer-drafted sentence hits `_get_judge().judge()` (strict_verify.py:516) → `_post_with_total_deadline` → `with _acquire_judge_slot():` (entailment_judge.py:155) before the POST. The writer wrapper's base_verify IS verify_sentence_provenance (abstractive_writer.py:409, confirmed via verified_compose.py), so the judge fires per sentence. (3) The judge builds its own `httpx.Client` (`_build_client`, entailment_judge.py:712/726) and BYPASSES OpenRouterClient/PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM entirely — so the 4-slot semaphore is the SOLE concurrency ceiling for judge POSTs, independent of PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16. This is a genuine, material throttle that caps the 0615bc5 speedup, so it must NOT be dismissed.

TWO corrections to the claim that I refuse to let stand as written but that do NOT falsify the finding: (a) The impact label "CRITICAL_serializes" is WRONG. The cap is 4 concurrent (measured =4), not 1. Serialization-to-1 is what finding 1 (the missing await) caused; this is a 4-way cap = a ~3x throttle at writer=12, not full serialization. (b) The claim's consequence (b) — "the thread doing sem.acquire() IS the event loop, so slot-queueing is loop-freeze" — is ALREADY VOID. The 0615bc5 fix wrapped `_draft_passes_wrapper` in `await asyncio.to_thread(...)` at abstractive_writer.py:833 and :912, so acquire() now runs on a worker thread, never the loop. Only consequence (a) survives. Since the central, load-bearing claim (a process-global BoundedSemaphore(4) caps in-flight NLI POSTs below the intended writer parallelism and thereby caps the pre-pass speedup) is true and reached and material, refuted=FALSE.
verdict.real_impact: Judge concurrency is hard-capped at 4 in-flight process-wide, versus PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 (iter5) or =24 (iter3/iter4). The verify leg is the majority of per-basket POSTs: each basket does 1 writer LLM call + S sequential per-sentence judge POSTs (S~3-5; _draft_passes_wrapper loops sentences serially, so one basket uses at most 1 slot at a time — the >4 demand materializes when >4 baskets are simultaneously in their verify phase, which holds most of the time since verify is the longer phase). Effective judge throughput is therefore 4/12 = 33% of intended at iter5 (~3x throttle), and 4/24 = ~6x throttle at iter3/iter4. At 346-basket scale that is ~1000-1700 judge POSTs; each is a high-effort glm-5.2 NLI call (code comments cite ~6-40s observed). Order-of-magnitude: ~1400 posts x ~10s / 4 ≈ 58 min for the verify leg vs / 12 ≈ 19 min — i.e. the 4-cap adds on the order of tens of minutes to a full run, and it caps the 0615bc5 pre-pass speedup regardless of the 12/24 writer setting. Cheap fix: set PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY to match the writer concurrency (12/24) in the launch scripts — the de-storm rationale (avoiding a 32-simultaneous 429 burst) is satisfied at 12 spread over 4 provider hosts (~3/host) and the slot is only held for the POST, not retry backoffs.

survivors.title: Every NLI verdict funnels through a process-global BoundedSemaphore(4) — the verify stage is hard-capped at ~0.9 verdicts/sec no matter what writer concurrency is set to
survivors.mechanism: DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4 builds a PROCESS-GLOBAL threading.BoundedSemaphore(4) (judge_concurrency.py:55,137-145). entailment_judge._post_with_total_deadline wraps the ENTIRE judge POST in `with _acquire_judge_slot():` (entailment_judge.py:155). Confirmed call chain: judge() :740 -> _judge_uncached() :765 -> _post_with_total_deadline() :999 -> :155 semaphore. Commit 0615bc5 moved this blocking POST off the event loop into asyncio.to_thread — so now up to 24 writer threads (PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12 x PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2) run concurrently and ALL of them block on this one 4-slot semaphore. NO launcher (launch_compose_gear_iter1/3/4/5.sh) ever sets PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY, so every real run used the default 4. The semaphore was added for a DIFFERENT workload (the per-claim credibility burst that 429-stormed friendli); the compose writer-verify path inherited it silently. Result: the 48-way parallelism the operator asked for is 4-way at the verify stage.
survivors.evidence:
MEASURED. Live probe of the real judge POST (z-ai/glm-5.2, production body: max_tokens=131072, reasoning effort=high, pinned mirror chain): median 4.4s/call, flat across span sizes. Then ran the REAL judge_concurrency module with the exact launcher env (PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY unset) and 24 threads each taking one 4.4s slot:
  DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4

verdict.reason:
The claim survives every refutation attempt. All mechanism points check out against the real code:

1. SEMAPHORE EXISTS & DEFAULTS TO 4 (verified empirically). judge_concurrency.py:55 `DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4`; `_get_semaphore` builds a process-global `threading.BoundedSemaphore(bound)` (:137-145). I ran the REAL module with launcher env (PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY unset): `resolved cap = 4`, and 24 threads yielded `peak concurrent slots = 4`, wall 1.20s vs 0.20s ideal = the exact 6x slowdown the investigator reported.

2. IT WRAPS THE POST. entailment_judge.py:155 `with _acquire_judge_slot():` wraps the entire blocking POST (imported from judge_concurrency at :99). Confirmed.

3. THE COMPOSE WRITER-VERIFY PATH REACHES IT. make_writer_verify_fn (abstractive_writer.py:384) → base_verify → verify_sentence_provenance (provenance_generator.py:2154) → `_get_judge().judge()` at :2558 → _post_with_total_deadline → :155 semaphore. The judge is gated on `_entailment_mode() in ("warn","enforce")` (:2540); default is "enforce" (strict_verify.py:355) AND iter4/iter5 launchers explicitly set PG_STRICT_VERIFY_ENTAILMENT=enforce — so the judge fires for every sentence passing mechanical checks.

4. NO OVERRIDE IN THE COMPOSE PATH. Grepped all launchers + src: no launcher sets PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY (default 4 holds; the dr_benchmark harness run_gate_b.py:1695 pins it even TIGHTER at 2). The only override, credibility_pass_concurrency (multi_section_generator.py:9527), is scoped to the ADVISORY credibility pass and is itself gated on PG_CREDIBILITY_PASS_SIDE_JUDGE_CONCURRENCY (unset → 0 → no-op); it does NOT wrap the writer-verify pre-pass.

5. THE 0615bc5 FIX MAKES IT BIND. Verify now runs via `await asyncio.to_thread(_draft_passes_wrapper, ...)` (abstractive_writer.py:833, 912), so up to ~24 writer threads run concurrently and all funnel through 4 slots. Before the fix the loop was serial (concurrency 1) so the semaphore was invisible; the fix exposes it as the next binding constraint — a textbook "fix one bottleneck, reveal the next" case.

The verdict cache does not refute: it is process-local in-memory (judge_verdict_cache.py:34) and cold on a fresh 346-basket render. The default asyncio executor cap (min(32,cpu+4)) sits ABOVE 4, so the semaphore remains the tighter cap. Single-process asyncio run (run_s5_i3.py:491 uses asyncio.Semaphore) confirms the semaphore is genuinely shared process-wide.
verdict.real_impact: A hard process-wide 4-way cap on ALL NLI verdicts during compose, independent of PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY (12-24) and PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS (2-3). At the investigator's 4.4s/call that is 4/4.4 = ~0.91 verdicts/sec ceiling. Reproduced 6.0x slowdown (24 requested → 4 admitted). At 346-basket scale, verify wall ≈ (total_verdicts × 4.4s)/4 regardless of writer fan-out; even at a conservative ~3 verdicts/basket (sentences + the re-anchor rescue second judge call at provenance_generator.py:2688) that is ~1038 verdicts → ~19 min of pure verify serialization, scaling linearly with sentences/basket. Amplifiers make it worse, not better: the launchers set PG_ENTAILMENT_TOTAL_S=300, so a single trickle-hang holds 1 of the 4 slots for up to 5 minutes (each concurrent hang cuts effective verify throughput by 25%), and the bounded same-provider retries (up to 3 attempts) each re-hold a slot. This is consistent with the corroborating wave evidence (12-15 baskets taking 428-2567s) being far larger than steady-state 0.91/sec alone predicts. Caveat on dominance: the writer LLM draft call is throttled separately (PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM 16-30) and is NOT under this semaphore, so verify (cap 4) is the tighter of the two stages; the semaphore is a real, reached, process-wide throughput floor, though hang/retry episodes (300s deadline) can co-dominate the tail. Fix is a one-line env: set PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY to match writer concurrency (the original 429-storm rationale was a different per-claim burst workload on friendli, not this writer-verify path).

survivors.title: The AUTHORITATIVE compose loop is a sync def called with no await from an async coroutine — the whole per-basket verify+redraft phase runs ON the event loop, concurrency 1. 0615bc5 fixed the pre-pass; this is the same bug one layer up and it is still at HEAD.
survivors.mechanism: `async def _run_section` (:5182) awaits `abstractive_pre_pass` (:5448 — now correctly parallel) and then calls `_vc_composed = _compose_section_per_basket(...)` at :5468 with NO await and NO asyncio.to_thread. `_compose_section_per_basket` is a plain `def` (verified_compose.py:3262). Inside it, per basket: (a) `verify_fn(sentence, pool)` = verify_sentence_provenance -> _get_judge().judge() (provenance_generator.py:2558) -> _post_with_total_deadline -> `fut.result(timeout=...)`, which BLOCKS THE CALLING THREAD; (b) `redraft_fn(...)` (verified_compose.py:2136) = the SYNTH_PRIMARY group re-draft, whose body does `fut = _ex.submit(lambda: asyncio.run(_run())); fut.result(timeout=call_deadline_s + 30.0)` (multi_section_generator.py:4994-4996) — a blocking wait of up to 330s on a FULL writer LLM call. The calling thread is the asyncio event loop. So for the entire compose phase of a section, the loop is frozen: no other section's pre-pass tasks run, no other basket's writer call runs, and even the judge's own 4-slot semaphore is irrelevant because only one judge call is ever issued at a time. The same defect exists on the non-abstractive branch at :5514. Commit 0615bc5 touched ONLY abstractive_writer.py (`git show --name-only`), so this path is untouched.

survivors.title: _compose_section_per_basket is a SYNC blocking basket loop called with no await/to_thread from the async _run_section - it serializes every per-basket NLI judge AND freezes the whole event loop (all other sections)
survivors.mechanism: _run_section is `async def`. At :5468 it calls `_vc_composed = _compose_section_per_basket(...)` as a plain function call. inspect.iscoroutinefunction(_compose_section_per_basket) == False. Its body loops `for basket in (section_baskets or [])` (verified_compose.py:3325) and, per basket, calls _compose_one_basket / compose_basket_multicited_synth_primary / compose_companion_figure_units (nested member x sentence-unit loop at :3414), each of which calls the SYNC verify_fn(sentence, scoped_pool) (verified_compose.py:2222, 2583, 2981, 3071, 3192) -> make_writer_verify_fn -> verify_sentence_provenance (provenance_generator.py:2154, sync) -> entailment_judge.judge() (sync) -> _post_with_total_deadline -> fut.result(timeout) which BLOCKS THE CALLING THREAD. The calling thread is the asyncio event loop. So (a) all per-basket verification in a section runs one judge POST at a time, and (b) for the entire duration the event loop is frozen, so every OTHER section's writer tasks, timeouts and HTTP callbacks make ZERO progress. This is exactly the 0615bc5 bug class, one level up, unfixed.
survivors.evidence: inspect probe: _compose_section_per_basket coroutine=False, _compose_one_basket coroutine=False, compose_companion_figure_units coroutine=False, verify_sentence_provenance coroutine=False; call site printed as `_vc_composed = _compose_section_per_basket(` with no await/to_thread (both sites). Section 2 DONE at 14:38:32 (log:10250); section 3's recovery pass then drafted 13/13 in 315s (log:10480, 14:41:43). survivors.fix_sketch: Wrap both call sites in `await asyncio.to_thread(_compose_section_per_basket, ...)` (unblocks the loop immediately, same as 0615bc5), then go further: make the per-basket loop fan out - either an async _compose_section_per_basket that gathers per-basket coroutines under a semaphore, or a bounded ThreadPoolExecutor over baskets with a copied contextvars context (the pattern already written for strict_verify at provenance_generator.py:3676-3718). Baskets are independent; verdicts are order-independent and are reassembled by index.

verdict.reason: The finding SURVIVES on its core, though its named mechanism is partly wrong. CONFIRMED: _run_section (multi_section_generator.py:5182) is async and its sibling section tasks run concurrently on ONE event loop (_gather_sections_isolated -> asyncio.ensure_future + asyncio.gather, :1003). _compose_section_per_basket is called at :5468/:5514 as a plain sync call with no await/to_thread; the code's own comment (abstractive_writer.py:4938-4940) admits it "runs synchronously inside the already-running section event loop" and executes the writer via a worker-thread asyncio.run that is JOINED — a join from the loop thread blocks the loop. The live log is a genuine smoking gun for the loop-freeze: while section 2 held the compose stage (14:02:38-14:38:32), section 3's concurrent pre-pass drafted 0/13 baskets across 1700s, then drafted 13/13 in 315s the moment section 2 released. 0 completions in 1700s vs 13 in 315s (~130x) is only explicable if sibling tasks got zero loop time — a freeze, not slow calls. HOWEVER the claim's stated mechanism "serializes every per-basket NLI judge" is REFUTED: there is a process-global cross-thread judge_verdict_cache (entailment_judge.py:740-762) that the concurrent pre-pass (_draft_passes_wrapper via asyncio.to_thread) pre-populates for every accepted sentence, so compose-phase verify_fn calls on the precomputed draft are cache hits, not POSTs. In the actual freeze window the 13 serial POSTs are large glm-5.2 WRITER generate calls (4.8-78.4s, thousands of reasoning tokens) from the synth_primary redraft_fn path, plus one 300s judge-deadline hang, not NLI judge calls. Net: the throughput cap (sync compose loop freezing all sibling sections) is real and file:line is right; the "NLI judge" attribution is wrong (it's the writer redraft + the redraft/join blocking pattern). Because the overall finding — _compose_section_per_basket freezes the event loop and destroys cross-section concurrency — holds and is well-measured, refuted=false, with the mechanism corrected.
verdict.real_impact: Cross-section (section-level) concurrency collapses to 1 while any legacy section is in its compose stage. Measured in iter4: section 2's compose stage held the event loop ~36 min (14:02:38-14:38:32); its sibling section 3 drafted 0 of 13 baskets in its full 1700s pre-pass wall, was force-abandoned to a K-span recovery pass, then drafted the same 13/13 in 315s once unblocked — i.e. ~1700s of a section's wall wasted to the freeze and the two legacy sections effectively serialized instead of overlapping. The dominant blocking unit is the synth_primary glm-5.2 writer redraft (asyncio.run+join in a worker thread, 5-78s each, run one at a time on the loop), NOT the NLI judge (cached). Fix value: wrapping _compose_section_per_basket in asyncio.to_thread restores cross-section overlap (it stays internally serial per its own baskets), but the bigger latent cost is that each redraft/writer call blocks the loop and glm-5.2 reasoning calls are 30-78s; the judge-side cost is already largely neutralized by the verdict cache.

survivors.title: coherence_rewrite_section is an async coroutine that calls the SYNC blocking verify_sentence_provenance once per sentence in a for-loop - blocking the event loop again

That is N_changed, not N_sentences, and is frequently 0 (rewrite no-op). It runs once per section (multi_section_generator.py:5591), gated on >=2 ev-token sentences (:247), across a handful of sections at default 3-way concurrency (Semaphore, :9937). So the surviving cost is at most tens of seconds of intermittent event-loop freeze aggregated over a whole report (each judge POST ~0.5-2s, starving up to 2 concurrent siblings), often near zero — versus the 346-basket primary compose verify loop which dominates. A trivial to_thread wrap (matching the sibling strict_verify at :5667 which IS wrapped) fixes it, but it is a LOW/secondary contributor, not the HIGH primary throughput cap claimed.
verdict.reason: The core mechanism is CONFIRMED by reading the real code: section_polish.py:283 calls the sync verify_sentence_provenance inside an async coroutine's for-loop (:271-291); that verifier invokes the entailment judge whose POST blocks via fut.result (entailment_judge.py:172); the judge is on by default (strict_verify.py:365 default 'enforce'); and unlike the sibling strict_verify at multi_section_generator.py:5667 this loop is NOT wrapped in asyncio.to_thread. Sections gather concurrently under a Semaphore (:9937, default 3), so a blocking verify does starve siblings. I therefore cannot refute the mechanism. However, the CLAIMED HIGH impact is overstated: verify fires only for CHANGED sentences (:274/:278), not all N; it is often a no-op (changed=0, zero judge calls); it runs once per section (:5591), gated on >=2 ev-token sentences (:247), over only a handful of sections — not per-basket. The bulk verify volume lives in the primary verified-compose writer path (e.g. :4177/:4913/:3913), which dominates. So the severity is refuted (LOW/secondary), while the bug itself is real and worth a 1-line to_thread fix.

(1) Path is reached: provenance_generator.verify_sentence_provenance:2558/2688 and strict_verify.py:516 call _get_judge().judge() -> _post_with_total_deadline -> `with _acquire_judge_slot():` (entailment_judge.py:155); iter5 sets PG_STRICT_VERIFY_ENTAILMENT=enforce so the per-sentence NLI POST is live. (2) Not guarded: PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY is unset in iter1/3/4/5 and run_s5_i3.py (appears only in a test + .codex docs), and the credibility_pass_concurrency override (multi_section_generator.py:9555) is a context manager scoped to the advisory credibility pass ONLY — compose writer/loop verify runs outside it, so resolve_max_concurrency() returns DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4. (3) Independent probe under env -i with the exact iter5 slate: resolve_max_concurrency()=4, 48 threads through the real acquire_judge_slot() -> PEAK=4, wall=3.00s (=48*0.25/4), reproducing the investigator's numbers. (4) The best refutation — 'judge just shares the 16-way PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM pool so cap-4 is moot' — is FALSE: entailment_judge builds its own httpx client and is throttled ONLY by the cap-4 slot, not the global LLM semaphore. So post-fix-#1 the writer generation runs 12/16-way while every sentence's NLI verify serializes through a SEPARATE isolated 4-way gate that has more work items (N sentences x 1-2 POSTs/basket) than the gen stage. Mechanism, reachability, env-resolution, and probe all hold.
verdict.real_impact: The compose-time NLI verify stage is hard-capped at 4 concurrent POSTs on its OWN pool (not the 16-way LLM pool), so after fix #1 the writer's 12-way parallelism cannot translate into more than 4 simultaneous sentence verifications. At 346-basket scale with ~1-4 sentences/basket and 1-2 judge POSTs/sentence (~2000+ POSTs; ledger shows 14764 judge rows total), raising the cap from 4 to ~12 would roughly 3x the throughput of the verify stage. Corroborated empirically: the cited DR session ran 2170 judge calls at achieved concurrency 3.94 pinned at the ceiling for 8208s (~2.3h) — at cap 12 that leg would drop to ~2700s, ~5500s saved. One caveat: whether verify (cap 4) or the large glm-5.2 writer generation (max_tokens 131072 / reasoning 65536 at 12-way) is THE single dominant post-#1 cost is not fully separable — they may be co-bottlenecks — but the verify cap is real, isolated, unguarded in the slate, and demonstrably binds in a real run. Fix is trivial/zero-risk: export PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY (or a compose-phase override) to match writer concurrency.

survivors.title: Phase-2 section assembly is a SYNCHRONOUS sequential per-basket loop -> NLI judge runs at concurrency 1 for 92% of wall-clock
survivors.mechanism: After the concurrent abstractive prepass (phase-1) finishes, _run_section calls the sync fn _compose_section_per_basket which does `for basket in section_baskets:` and, per basket, composes+verifies each sentence via verify_fn (the NLI entailment judge, an OpenRouter LLM POST) plus bounded redraft repairs, strictly one basket and one sentence at a time. Nothing fans this out. The prepass concurrency knobs (PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12, PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16) do NOT apply to this phase, so the missing-await fix bought nothing here.
survivors.evidence: Instrumented run, section 0, cap-primary=6, wall=735.2s. Monkeypatched httpx: judge(sync NLI) = 105 POSTs, busy 673.0s (median 5.52s, all HTTP 200); writer(async) = 21 POSTs, busy only 20.4s. Sampler @0.25s: judge sync-inflight pinned at exactly 1 (avg 0.92, judge_busy=82.95%) from t~16s to t=735s. writer avg inflight 0.03. Combined 0.17 req/s. Extrapolation: 123s/primary-basket serial; at judge concurrency 4 the phase drops ~673s->168s (run ~230s), at 12 ~56s (run ~118s) = a 3-13x win.
survivors.fix_sketch: Fan out the per-basket verify/redraft in _compose_section_per_basket concurrently (asyncio.gather over baskets, or run each basket's verify via a bounded pool), so multiple NLI judge POSTs are in flight at once instead of 1.

verdict.reason: Could not kill it; confirmed by code + their measurement. (1) Reached in production: all launch_compose_gear_*.sh set PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY=1 + PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER=1, so _run_section takes the abstractive branch and _compose_section_per_basket (verified_compose.py:3262, loop :3325) is the primary body producer. (2) It is a bare SYNC call — multi_section_generator.py:5468/5514 call it with NO await and NO to_thread inside `async def _run_section` (:5182). (3) verify_fn (_vc_writer_verify -> make_writer_verify_fn -> verify_sentence_provenance -> _get_judge().judge() -> _post_with_total_deadline -> fut.result(), entailment_judge.py:172) is a BLOCKING single-worker POST executed on the event-loop thread; no fan-out, strictly serial per sentence per basket. (4) The 0615bc5 missing-await fix was in the PRE-PASS (_draft_passes_wrapper wrapped in await asyncio.to_thread at abstractive_writer.py:833/912); phase-2 is untouched, so that fix bought nothing here — matches the claim. Refutation attempts failed: section concurrency (Semaphore=3 default at :9937) does NOT parallelize this because the bare sync call blocks the whole event loop through every ~5.5s POST, so phase-2 serializes LOOP-WIDE across sections, not just within one — worse than stated. The default-ON verdict cache (entailment_judge.py:756) does not save it empirically: the investigator measured 105 real HTTP-200 judge POSTs busy 673s in phase-2 (plausibly because phase-2 re-resolves spans, changing the cache key vs the pre-pass). Not dominated by a bigger cost: judge busy 673.0s vs writer 20.4s of 735.2s wall (91.5%), sync-inflight pinned at ~1 for 82.95% of wall.
verdict.real_impact: In the measured section: judge verification is 673.0s of 735.2s wall-clock (91.5%), running at achieved concurrency ~0.92 (effectively 1) via 105 serial ~5.5s POSTs. Because _compose_section_per_basket is a bare sync call blocking the event loop, this serialization is loop-wide, so neither PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=3 nor the missing-await fix helps it. Fanning the judge out (or offloading the loop per-section to worker threads so section concurrency actually applies) cuts the judge-bound phase ~3-12x (673s->~168s at 4, ~56s at 12), consistent with their extrapolation. Caveat: the full 346-basket projection assumes uniform judge-POST density across baskets, which I could not independently verify; the confirmed part is the per-section serialization and ~90% wall-clock-at-concurrency-1.

survivors.title: _compose_section_per_basket runs synchronously ON the event-loop thread (no await / no to_thread) -> blocks the loop for the entire section, defeating PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS
survivors.mechanism: async def _run_section calls the blocking sync _compose_section_per_basket with NO await and NO asyncio.to_thread (code comment at multi_section_generator.py:4938 even states it 'runs synchronously inside the already-running section event loop'). Each internal NLI judge POST blocks on concurrent.futures fut.result(timeout=...) on the loop thread. So while section N is in phase-2, the event loop cannot advance any other coroutine — including a second section's phase-2. PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS=2 therefore cannot overlap section bodies; on a 4-section/346-basket run all section assemblies serialize end-to-end.
survivors.evidence: Heartbeat coroutine (await asyncio.sleep(0.01) loop) recorded max_gap_s=631.09 — the loop did not tick for 631 consecutive seconds during phase-2 (t~78s..709s), vs 0.01s target. Only 3 gaps >0.05s total, the big one spanning the whole verify phase. This proves the loop is blocked, not merely idle-awaiting.
survivors.fix_sketch: Wrap the phase-2 call in asyncio.to_thread (like the prepass fix did for _draft_passes_wrapper) so sections' phase-2 work runs off-loop and two sections can genuinely overlap; combined with Finding 1's intra-section fan-out.

verdict.reason:
Every refutation angle failed. (1) Reachability: the sync path IS reached at runtime — probe.log shows section 0 entering it with PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY ON. (2) Not guarded elsewhere: _compose_section_per_basket (verified_compose.py:3262) is called bare (no await, no to_thread) at multi_section_generator.py:5468/5514 from async _run_section; its own docstring at :4938 admits it "runs synchronously inside the already-running section event loop," and the redraft closure _make_group_redraft_fn (:4993) blocks the loop on fut.result(). (3) Same-loop parallelism: _run_legacy_bounded (:10115) runs `async with sem: await _run_section` under asyncio.Semaphore + asyncio.gather — sections share ONE event loop, so a sync block freezes all siblings. (4) Impact is real and NOT dominated: probe.log records max_gap_s=631.09 spanning t≈78.8s→709.9s — the loop did not tick for the entire compose phase (86% of the 734s section), while the awaited pre-pass (78s) and awaited strict_verify (~24s tail, to_thread) tick fine. My strongest counter — that the judge_verdict_cache would make the re-verify cache-warm and cheap — is defeated because PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY's group-redraft loop makes 9 fresh blocking glm writer calls + ~87 NLI POSTs inside the sync window (uncacheable, all on the loop thread). Minor imprecisions in the claim (dominant cost is the redraft writer calls, not strictly NLI POSTs; the env var is PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS not PG_MAX_PARALLEL_SECTIONS) do not change the serialization conclusion.</parameter>
<parameter name="real_impact">Measured: the sync _compose_section_per_basket blocks the shared event loop for 631s per section (86% of the 734s single-section probe; pre-pass is only 78s, strict_verify ~24s). Because all sections run on one loop via asyncio.gather + Semaphore(3 default), each section's ~631s compose fully freezes the loop, so the compose phases of a 4-section/346-basket run serialize end-to-end (~4×631s ≈ 42 min that PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS is supposed to overlap but cannot) — and even peer sections' pre-passes cannot interleave during a sibling's compose block. Fix: dispatch _compose_section_per_basket via await asyncio.to_thread(...) at both call sites (:5468, :5514), exactly like the pre-pass fix in commit 0615bc5 and the strict_verify call at :5667 already do. Gated on PG_SYNTH_PRIMARY/PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER being ON (they are, in the measured config and per the code's 'primary body path' comments); with synth_primary OFF the redraft writer calls vanish and cache-warm verification would shrink the block substantially.</parameter>
</invoke>

survivors.title: NLI/entailment judge path is capped at 4 concurrent (PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY default=4) and is NOT gated by the writer's global Semaphore(16) — the ceiling once serialization is fixed
survivors.mechanism: Every entailment POST goes through acquire_judge_slot() -> a process-global threading.BoundedSemaphore(4). The writer async calls use a SEPARATE asyncio.Semaphore(PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16) in providers/llm_provider.py and never touch the judge slot. So the two OpenRouter paths have independent, unequal caps. Today the 4-slot cap is moot (serial caller only ever holds 1), but it becomes the binding limit the moment Findings 1/2 are fixed to fan out verification — verification would parallelize to 4, not 12/16.
survivors.evidence: Sampler read judge BoundedSemaphore._value: sat at 3 (=1 in use) essentially the whole run, dipping to 4-free/0-in-use only in inter-basket gaps — never below 0-free, confirming the 4-cap was never approached because the caller is serial. Slate does not set PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY, so default 4 is active.
survivors.fix_sketch: When enabling concurrent phase-2 verification, also raise PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY to match the intended writer concurrency (e.g. 12-16), and consider unifying the judge under the same global LLM semaphore for one coherent account-wide cap.

verdict.reason: Could not refute. Every load-bearing element of the claim is confirmed by reading the real code and by a measured probe. (1) judge_concurrency.py DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4 and resolve_max_concurrency() returns 4 when unset; a 12-thread probe admitted a PEAK of exactly 4 concurrent judge slots. (2) entailment_judge.py:155 wraps every judge POST in _acquire_judge_slot() — a process-global threading.BoundedSemaphore, distinct from the writer's PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM asyncio semaphore in src/providers/llm_provider.py:79. Independent, unequal caps: confirmed. (3) Path is definitely reached: the compose slate (launch_compose_gear_iter3/4/5.sh) sets PG_STRICT_VERIFY_ENTAILMENT=enforce, and abstractive_writer.py:1136 hard-errors unless enforce, so _get_judge().judge() fires on every accepted sentence (provenance_generator.py:2558). (4) The slate does NOT set PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY (only scripts/dr_benchmark/run_gate_b.py sets it, to 2 — a different harness), so default 4 is live. (5) The judge model is z-ai/glm-5.2 — the SAME expensive reasoning model as the writer — so each of the ~2-per-sentence judge POSTs is comparable in cost to a writer call, making the 4-cap plausibly co-dominant/dominant post-serialization, not negligible. I tried to refute on 'entailment off by default' (module default IS off, but slate forces enforce), on 'env overridden' (not in the compose slate), on 'dominated by writer' (aggregate judge work ~ writer work due to glm-5.2 + double verification), and on 'not reached' (it is) — all failed. Claim survives.
verdict.real_impact: Post-serialization, verification fan-out is hard-capped at 4 concurrent glm-5.2 judge POSTs regardless of writer concurrency (12) or PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM (16-30) — measured peak=4 with 12 waiters. Judge is invoked on every accepted sentence, ~2 passes (pre-pass proposal + authoritative compose gate), model z-ai/glm-5.2 at ~6-40s/call. Rough 346-basket aggregate: writer ~69k s-work at 12-16 wide ≈ 4.3-5.8k s wall; judge ~60k s-work at 4 wide ≈ 15k s wall. If pipelined, the judge 4-cap is the binding wall and raising it toward 12-16 would cut judge wall ~3x (~15k→~5k s), i.e. it becomes the dominant ceiling once serialization is fixed. Caveat on the REMEDY (not the finding): the _live_run log shows the judge path 429-storming the SAME OpenRouter account; naively raising the cap risks re-triggering the account-QPS 429s the de-storm cap was added to prevent, so realized speedup depends on shared-account headroom.

survivors.title: Rate limiting (429s) and API errors are NOT currently a throughput cap in this account/window
survivors.mechanism: Every OpenRouter POST (writer + judge) returned HTTP 200; no 429 backoff sleeps, no retries, no crashes were triggered during the run, so none of the wall-clock is retry/backoff. The slowness is pure serialization, not scarcity.
survivors.evidence: HTTP_429_count=0 of 126 POSTs; status_codes {200:21} writer, {200:105} judge. NOTE/CONTAMINATION CAVEAT: the root-owned run_s5_i3.py PID 966951 was ALIVE the entire time (ELAPSED 02:47:18, --cap-primary 0, same OpenRouter account) — yet I still saw zero 429s, so account contention was not biting in this window; a heavier concurrent load could differ. The absolute req/s (0.17) is therefore a floor set by serialization, not by the provider.
survivors.fix_sketch: No action on rate limiting now; re-measure 429 rate AFTER the serialization fixes raise in-flight concurrency, since that is when account QPS could start to bind.

verdict.reason: Tried to kill the claim by measuring a much larger sample than the investigator's 126 POSTs: the live root-owned compose run's own log (/workspace/compose_wt/outputs/s5_gear_iter4/compose.log), 4639 OpenRouter POSTs over the full 08:33-15:58 (~7.4h) window on the SAME account. Result confirms the claim rather than refuting it: 4627x HTTP 200, 12x HTTP 429 (0.26%), zero other status codes, zero ERROR/judge_error/error_unexpected lines. Every 429 is recovered ('rate-limit backoff 15.0s' then a successful retry). Total backoff cost = 12 x 15.0s = 180s = 0.68% of run wall-clock. The backoff is time.sleep (entailment_judge.py:1166/1209) but runs inside asyncio.to_thread (post-0615bc5), and the POST semaphore slot is explicitly released during backoff (entailment_judge.py:151), so one judge's 429 wait blocks neither the event loop nor its 11 sibling judges — no amplification. The claim that 429s/API errors are NOT a throughput cap (impact LOW) holds up under a larger, independent measurement; I could not find a mechanism that makes it a cap.
verdict.real_impact: Rate limiting costs ~180s of thread-local backoff over ~7.4h of runtime (0.68% of wall-clock), fully recovered (all 12 429s retried to 200), non-amplifying (semaphore released during backoff, sleep is off the event loop). Negligible vs the serialization/latency costs; not a throughput cap in this account/window.

result.synthesis:
Note: claude-opus-4-8[1m] (the safety classifier) was unavailable when reviewing this subagent's work. Please carefully verify the subagent's actions and output before acting on them.

Spot-checks confirm the two load-bearing facts at HEAD in /home/polaris/wt/compose: `_compose_section_per_basket` is still called bare (no await/to_thread) at src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514, and `DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY = 4` at src/polaris_graph/llm/judge_concurrency.py:55 with `PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY` set in zero launch scripts (grep exit 1).

WHY COMPOSE IS SLOW — SYNTHESIS OF SURVIVING FINDINGS

1. RANKED CAUSES (real cost at 346-basket scale)

#1 — The compose phase itself still runs ON the event loop. [MEASURED, dominant]
src/polaris_graph/generator/multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514: `async def _run_section` calls the sync `_compose_section_per_basket` (verified_compose.py:3262) with no await and no to_thread. Inside it, every per-sentence verify blocks on `fut.result()` and — worse — the SYNTH_PRIMARY redraft (`_make_group_redraft_fn`, multi_section_generator.py:4994-4996) blocks the loop on a FULL glm-5.2 writer call, up to 330s per firing, up to 2x per failed basket. 0615bc5 fixed this bug class in the pre-pass only; this is the same bug one layer up, unfixed at HEAD.
Measured: heartbeat probe showed the loop did not tick for 631 consecutive seconds during one section's compose (86% of a 734s section); instrumented section 0: judge busy 673.0s of 735.2s wall at inflight pinned to 1; iter4 log: section 3 drafted 0/13 baskets in 1700s while section 2 held the loop, then 13/13 in 315s once released; section 0 total 3266s of which only 591s (pre-pass) was parallel — 82% of the section at concurrency 1. This is the reason PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_CONCURRENCY=12, PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16, and PG_PARALLEL_SECTIONS are all inert during compose. Cost: the majority of the measured ~7.4h iter4 wall.

#2 — Process-global NLI judge semaphore hard-capped at 4. [MEASURED, becomes binding the moment #1 is fixed]
judge_concurrency.py:55 DEFAULT_MAX_CONCURRENCY=4; entailment_judge.py:155 wraps every judge POST in `_acquire_judge_slot()`; PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY is set in no launcher (verified again just now). The judge uses its own httpx client, so PG_MAX_CONCURRENT_LLM=16 never applies to it. Measured: probes admit peak exactly 4 with 24/48 waiters (6.0x slowdown vs unbounded); a real ledger session ran 2170 judge calls at achieved concurrency 3.94 for 8208s. At ~4.4-5.5s/verdict and ~1000-2000 verdicts per 346-basket run, cap 4 puts a ~19-46 min floor under the verify leg; cap 12 cuts that ~3x. Today it is masked by #1 (a serial caller never needs more than 1 slot).

#3 — sentence_repair: awaited one-at-a-time repair loop + sync verify on the loop. [MEASURED counts, MEDIUM]
sentence_repair.py:408/:428/:519. ~90-99 repair attempts per run across ~10-11 sections, each a serialized glm-5.2 reasoning call plus a loop-blocking NLI POST. Bounded per-section (cap 10), does not scale with baskets; order of 1-2 min of loop freeze plus serialized repair tails. Fix after #1/#2.

#4 — coherence_rewrite_section sync verify in async loop. [CODE-READ only, LOW]
section_polish.py:271-283. Real bug, same class, but fires only on changed sentences, once per section — tens of seconds per run at most. Refuter downgraded from HIGH.

NOT a cause — 429s / API errors. [MEASURED] 12 of 4639 POSTs were 429 (0.26%), all recovered, 180s total backoff = 0.68% of wall. The original "429 is the ceiling" thesis remains dead — with one caveat under gap (5).

2. THE OPERATOR'S THREE HYPOTHESES, BY NAME

- CONTEXT TOO LARGE? NO, on current evidence. Judge latency measured flat across span sizes (median 4.4-5.5s); single writer calls measure 7-11s live. Nothing in eight lanes surfaced an unbounded prompt-concatenation mechanism. Caveat: nobody actually counted prompt tokens per call — this is a "no confirmed mechanism + flat latency" no, not a token-count no.
- TOKEN BUDGET? NO — the opposite. No truncation finding survived. Budgets are enormous (PG_ABSTRACTIVE_WRITER_MAX_TOKENS=131072, reasoning 65536), which makes each *serialized* redraft call take 30-78s. Budget is not starving compose; oversized reasoning budgets amplify the serialization cost of #1.
- CRASHES? NO. Zero errors/exceptions in the measured windows; all 12 429s retried to 200; no abandonments except the pre-pass wall-deadline event, which was itself CAUSED by #1 (a starved sibling section), not a crash.

The real answer to "what else is capping compose" is: the same missing-await bug class, twice more (compose phase and its redraft path), plus a 4-slot judge semaphore inherited from a different workload.

3. THE ONE THING
Wrap both call sites in `await asyncio.to_thread(_compose_section_per_basket, ...)` at multi_section_generator.py:5468 and :5514 (functools.partial for kwargs). This unfreezes the loop: sections genuinely overlap, sibling pre-passes stop starving, and the redraft's inner `asyncio.run` (already on its own worker thread) becomes correct off-loop. Expected speedup: the measured runs spent ~82-86% of section wall at concurrency 1 because of this; restoring 2-3-way section overlap plus intra-run pipelining is a realistic 3-6x on total wall. Strongly recommended rider (one line, zero code): export PG_SIDE_JUDGE_MAX_CONCURRENCY=12 in the launcher — otherwise the to_thread fix immediately hits the 4-slot judge wall and you capture only part of the win.

4. HONEST WALL-CLOCK AFTER THE TOP FIX
Baseline: 346 baskets took ~7.4h (iter4). With the to_thread fix ALONE: verify floor = ~1000-2000 judge POSTs x ~5s / 4 slots ≈ 21-42 min, plus writer/redraft legs at 12-16 wide ≈ 10-25 min, partially pipelined — estimate ~1 to 1.5 hours. With the judge cap also raised to 12: verify floor drops to ~7-15 min — estimate ~30-60 minutes. This rests on four assumptions: (a) judge-POST density ~3-5/basket holds across all 346 baskets (measured on sections 0-3 only); (b) the redraft/verify-failure rate stays at observed levels (not measured at full scale); (c) no 429 storm at true 12-16-way concurrency (429s were only measured under serialization — see gaps); (d) per-call latencies (judge ~5s, writer 7-30s) hold. Treat 30-90 min as the honest band, not a point estimate.

5. WHAT WE DID NOT ESTABLISH
- Verify-failure/redraft frequency at 346-basket scale. The 330s-per-redraft path is confirmed live, but how many of 346 baskets trigger it is unmeasured; it swings the estimate by tens of minutes.
- 429 behavior at real concurrency. "429 is not a cap" was measured at ~0.17 req/s effective — i.e., while serialized. At genuine 12-16-way on one OpenRouter account it may bind; must be re-measured after the fix.
- The verdict-cache contradiction. One measured lane saw 105 real phase-2 judge POSTs (cache misses); another verdict says compose-phase verifies are cache hits from the pre-pass. The cache-key mismatch (span re-resolution?) is unexplained; it determines whether phase-2 verify cost is ~0 or ~600s/section post-fix.
- Writer-vs-judge dominance post-fix. Whether generation (16-way pool) or verify (judge pool) is the binding stage after both fixes is unresolved; they may co-bottleneck.
- Actual prompt token counts. The "context not too large" answer is inferential; no one measured tokens-per-call across basket sizes.
- No end-to-end A/B of the fixed build at 346 baskets exists yet. Everything above extrapolates from 4-15-basket probes and the iter4 log.

summary: Line-by-line Opus investigation of what ACTUALLY caps compose throughput. 8 hypothesis lenses, adversarial verification, Fable synthesis. Find every serialization bug, not just the one we already know.
